summary: Exact attendance percentages require accounting for every user and registration, and the mandated order requires sorting the c contest rows; hash aggregation followed by sorting matches those input and comparison-order lower bounds.

optimality.argument: A correct result depends on the total user count and every registration row, requiring Omega(u+r) input work. For arbitrary contest percentages and IDs, producing c rows in the required two-key order has an Omega(c log c) comparison lower bound. Hash aggregation scans Register once and sorting the c groups meets the combined bound.

replacement_checks.evidence: Changing any Users row can change the denominator, changing any Register row can change a contest numerator, and arbitrary contest percentages require comparison sorting of c output groups.
The scalar user count and grouped registration counts take O(u+r) scan work with hash aggregation, followed by O(c log c) ordering and O(c) projection.

Regression compares query output with an independent counter oracle across full attendance, unregistered users, one-sixth and two-thirds rounding, shared users, and percentage ties ordered by contest ID.
